title: Equivalent Expressions: Graphing and Slider Methods in Desmos
description: Two Desmos-based strategies for equivalent-expression questions — graph-and-match for one variable, and sliders for several variables — plus when each one applies.

blocks.html:
Equivalent Expressions with Desmos

Some Advanced Math questions hand you an expression or equation and ask, "Which choice is equivalent?" You can always grind through the algebra — but when there are answer choices to compare against, Desmos gives you two fast, reliable shortcuts.

  • The graphing method — best when there is just one variable.
  • The slider method — best when there are two or more variables.

By the end of this lesson you'll know how to run each one and, just as important, when not to.

Method 1: Graph and Match

The idea is simple. Graph the original equation, then graph each answer choice one at a time. The choice whose graph lands exactly on top of the original is the equivalent one.

Two expressions are equivalent precisely when they produce the same output for every input — so their graphs are identical. That's what you're looking for: a perfect overlap, not just a similar shape.

Let's try it on a real question:

Which expression is equivalent to \(\dfrac{4}{4x-5}-\dfrac{1}{x+1}\)?

One of the choices is \(\dfrac{9}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\). Let's graph the original against that choice and see if they match.

blocks.prompt_html: We'll test the choice \(\dfrac{9}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\) against the original. Follow the steps to enter both graphs and compare them.
blocks.correct_html: Exactly. The two graphs sit perfectly on top of each other, so \(\dfrac{9}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\) is equivalent to the original. That's the answer.
blocks.incorrect_html: Look again — every point of the candidate curve falls on the original curve, including the breaks at \(x=-1\) and \(x=\tfrac{5}{4}\). A complete overlap like this means the expressions are equivalent.

blocks.html:
That's the whole technique. In a real test you'd keep the original on screen and type in each choice in turn; three of them will miss, and one will vanish right onto the original curve. That one is your answer.

Now the fine print — the three things that make or break this method.

Rule 1: The variable can always be \(x\)

Desmos graphs in terms of \(x\). If a question is written with a different letter — say \(t\) or \(n\) — that's no problem: the letter is just a placeholder. You're free to rewrite the whole equation with \(x\) in its place, then graph as usual. Renaming the variable never changes whether two expressions are equivalent.

blocks.question_html: A question asks which expression is equivalent to \(\dfrac{3}{t-2}+\dfrac{1}{t}\), using the variable \(t\). You'd like to graph it. What should you do?

choices.text: Skip the graphing method — it only works when the question literally uses \(x\).
Rewrite every \(t\) as \(x\), graph \(\dfrac{3}{x-2}+\dfrac{1}{x}\), and compare the choices the same way.
Graph it with \(t\) on the horizontal axis instead of \(x\).

blocks.correct_html: Right. The variable's name is arbitrary, so swap \(t\) for \(x\) everywhere and graph normally.
blocks.incorrect_html: Not quite. Desmos graphs in terms of \(x\), but the variable in the problem is just a placeholder. Replace every \(t\) with \(x\) — in the original and in the choices — and the method works exactly as before.
blocks.rejoin_html: Takeaway: a single variable under any name can be renamed to \(x\) for graphing.

blocks.html:
Rule 2: Only one variable

Graphing on the \(xy\)-plane shows one input (\(x\)) against one output (\(y\)). The moment an expression has two or more variables, there's no single curve to overlap, and the graph-and-match trick falls apart. When you spot a second variable, stop and reach for the slider method instead (coming up next).

blocks.question_html: You need an expression equivalent to \(5a^2b - 10ab^2\), which contains the variables \(a\) and \(b\). Is the graph-and-match method a good fit?

choices.text: Yes — graph it like any other expression and look for the overlap.
No — there are two variables, so graphing won't give a single curve to compare. Use the slider method.

blocks.correct_html: Correct. Two variables means no single \(x\)-vs-\(y\) curve, so graph-and-match doesn't apply. Sliders are the move.
blocks.incorrect_html: Reconsider. With both \(a\) and \(b\) in play there's no single curve to overlay, so graphing breaks down. This is exactly the case the slider method is built for.
blocks.rejoin_html: Takeaway: one variable → graph; two or more → sliders.

blocks.html:
Rule 3: You need answer choices to compare against

Both Desmos methods work by matching — you compare the original to each choice. If a question simply says "simplify" or "rewrite" with no choices listed (a student-produced-response question, for instance), there's nothing to overlay against, and there's no way to know when you've matched. In that situation, fall back on algebra.

blocks.question_html: A grid-in (no answer choices) question asks you to write \(\dfrac{x^2-9}{x+3}\) in simplest form. Should you graph and match?

choices.text: Yes — graph it and read the answer off the screen.
No — with no choices to compare against, there's nothing to match, so use algebra instead.

blocks.correct_html: Exactly. Matching needs something to match against. With no choices, simplify by hand (here, \(x-3\)).
blocks.incorrect_html: Think about what "match" means. Without answer choices there's no second graph to overlap, so the method has nothing to confirm. Simplify algebraically instead.
blocks.rejoin_html: Takeaway: no answer choices → no matching → use another method.

blocks.html:
Method 2: Sliders for Several Variables

When an expression carries two or more variables, graphing stalls — even if the variables happen to be \(x\) and \(y\). Instead, turn each variable into a slider and compare values.

The plan:

  1. Type the original expression into Desmos and let it create a slider for each variable.
  2. Type each answer choice on its own line.
  3. Read off the numerical value of each. The choice that produces the same value as the original is the equivalent one.

We'll use this question:

Which expression is equivalent to \(6x^8y^2+12x^2y^2\)?

One catch: Desmos won't make sliders for \(x\) and \(y\)

Desmos treats \(x\) and \(y\) as graphing axes, so it refuses to turn them into sliders. The fix is to rename them to something adjacent — capital \(X\) and \(Y\) work perfectly. So before entering our question, rewrite

\(6x^8y^2+12x^2y^2\) as \(6X^8Y^2+12X^2Y^2\),

and rewrite every answer choice the same way. Now Desmos will happily build an \(X\) slider and a \(Y\) slider.

blocks.prompt_html: Enter the original expression (with capital \(X\) and \(Y\)), create a slider for each, then we'll test the choices against it.

blocks.html:
With the sliders set, type each rewritten choice on its own line and watch the values:

  • \(6X^2Y^2(2X^6)\)
  • \(6X^2Y^2(X^4)\)
  • \(6X^2Y^2(X^6+2)\)
  • \(6X^2Y^2(X^4+2)\)

Only \(6X^2Y^2(X^6+2)\) reports the same value as the original for the slider settings you chose. That's the equivalent expression — the answer.

Always move the sliders off 1

Here's the trap. If every slider sits at its default value of \(1\), several different expressions can accidentally produce the same number — a coincidence, not real equivalence. To rule that out, drag each slider to a different, random value (try \(X=3\), \(Y=-2\), for example). A choice that still matches at messy, unequal values is genuinely equivalent, not just a lucky coincidence at \(1\).

blocks.question_html: Why should you move the sliders away from \(1\) (and to different values from each other) before deciding which choice matches?

choices.text: Desmos can't evaluate an expression while a slider is set to 1.
At \(1\) (or equal values) a wrong choice can coincidentally equal the original; varied values expose those impostors.
Moving sliders makes the graph easier to see.

blocks.correct_html: Right. Distinct, non-1 values break coincidental matches, so only a truly equivalent choice keeps up.
blocks.incorrect_html: The concern is false matches. At \(1\) — or any shared value — a non-equivalent choice can land on the same number by accident. Spreading the sliders to different, non-1 values weeds those out.
blocks.rejoin_html: Takeaway: random, distinct slider values prevent coincidental matches.

blocks.question_html: A multi-variable question is written with \(x\) and \(y\). To use the slider method, what do you do first?

choices.text: Nothing special — Desmos makes sliders for \(x\) and \(y\) like any other letter.
Replace \(x\) and \(y\) with capital \(X\) and \(Y\) (in the original and every choice) so Desmos will create sliders.
Switch to the graphing method instead.

blocks.correct_html: Correct. Desmos reserves \(x\) and \(y\) for the axes, so rename them to \(X\) and \(Y\) everywhere first.
blocks.incorrect_html: Desmos won't turn \(x\) or \(y\) into sliders — they're the graphing axes. Rename them to capital \(X\) and \(Y\) (consistently across the original and all choices) and the sliders appear.

blocks.question_html: The slider method shares one big limitation with the graphing method. Which is it?

choices.text: It only works when the expression has exactly two variables.
It needs answer choices to compare against — with nothing to match, there's no way to confirm a result.

blocks.correct_html: Exactly. Like graphing, the slider method is a matching tool — no choices, no match. Use algebra there.
blocks.incorrect_html: Sliders handle any number of variables and many expression types. The real limit is the same as before: you need answer choices to compare against. Without them, fall back on algebra.
blocks.rejoin_html: Takeaway: both Desmos methods require answer choices to compare against.

blocks.html:
Choosing your method

Quick decision guide for the next equivalent-expression question you see:

  • One variable + answer choices? Graph and match. Rename the variable to \(x\) if needed.
  • Two or more variables + answer choices? Use sliders. Rename \(x,y\) to \(X,Y\), and move every slider to a different, non-1 value.
  • No answer choices? Neither Desmos trick applies — there's nothing to match, so solve it with algebra.

Pick the tool that fits what's in front of you, and these questions become fast and nearly mistake-proof.
